title: Hanwha Semitech Nears Supply of Hybrid Bonder to SK Hynix - thelec.net
summary: Hanwha Semitech generated more than 90 billion won in revenue last year from its TC bonder SFM5 Expert model, with SK Hynix as a key customer. The company previously supplied a first-generation hybrid bonder to SK Hynix in 2022 for research and development purposes. Samsung Electronics, meanwhile, is said to be evaluating a die-to-wafer (D2W) hybrid bonder developed by its affiliate Semes. The adoption of Hanwha Semitech's hybrid bonder in mass production will depend on its performance and cost competitiveness. The SHB2 Nano is believed to have been developed faster than Prodrive Technologies' typical timelines, raising concerns about potential completeness, according to people familiar with the project. [...] Join us UPDATED. 2026-03-26 07:41 (목) Hanwha Semitech Nears Supply of Hybrid Bonder to SK Hynix Hanwha Semitech Nears Supply of Hybrid Bonder to SK Hynix JEONG IL JOO 승인 2026.03.26 07:39 댓글 0 이 기사를 공유합니다 Second-Generation System Developed With Prodrive Technologies Enters Qualification Phase Hanwha Semitech is expected to supply its second-generation hybrid bonding equipment to SK Hynix as early as next month, marking a potential step toward entering advanced semiconductor packaging production lines. The company recently brought its "SHB2 Nano" hybrid bonder into South Korea after assembling the system in the Netherlands and plans to begin qualification testing with SK Hynix, according to people familiar with the matter. [...] In addition, collaboration with Prodrive Technologies is likely to have increased development costs compared with in-house production, market observers said. Besi's hybrid bonders are priced at around 5 billion to 6 billion won per unit. Industry estimates suggest that domestically developed hybrid bonders could be priced at around 4 billion won, positioning them between TC bonders and Besi's systems. 저작권자 © THE ELEC, Korea Electronics Industry Media 무단전재 및 재배포 금지 JEONG IL JOO 다른기사 보기 댓글삭제 삭제한 댓글은 다시 복구할 수 없습니다. 그래도 삭제하시겠습니까? 댓글 0 댓글쓰기 계정을 선택하시면 로그인·계정인증을 통해 댓글을 남기실 수 있습니다. 회원 로그인 --- 최신순 추천순 ---

title: 2026 South Korea Private Capital Breakdown - PitchBook
summary: Several structural forces continue to shape investment opportunities across South Korea’s private markets. Corporate carveouts driven by chaebol restructuring have become a notable source of private equity deal flow, while corporate venture capital accounted for more than one-third of venture transactions and over 60% of total deal value in 2025. Fundraising trends also highlight a widening divide between asset classes: Private equity raised $8.4 billion across just four funds in 2025—nearly 8x the capital raised by venture vehicles—illustrating the increasing institutionalization of South Korea’s buyout market even as the venture ecosystem undergoes a cyclical reset. Table of contents | | | --- | | Market overview | 3 | | Dealmaking | 5 | | Exits | 16 | | Fundraising | 20 | [...] Private equity deal value declined in 2025 even as the number of transactions increased slightly, highlighting the changing composition of deal activity within South Korea’s buyout market. title: James Todd & Co warn of financial blind spots in consultancy sector - Yahoo News UK
summary: Advertisement Advertisement Advertisement Advertisement Concerns about how and where AI should or can be used successfully, with 39 per cent of firms stating that they have encountered problems identifying the right applications. Costs, with barriers for smaller or earlier-stage organisations impacting 21 per cent of businesses. 88 per cent of firms in the top ten per cent of management practice datasets have adopted at least some AI applications, compared to just 51 per cent of those in the lowest ten per cent . Security and data protection, with AI still a relatively new and rapidly evolving technology with long-term ramifications not fully understood, making cybersecurity just as relevant. [...] Advertisement Advertisement Advertisement Advertisement Oliver Read, FCCA ACA, Audit and Assurance Director from James Todd & Co explains, "AI and other machine learning tech have, of course, had major ramifications for almost every sector, and in professional services we’ve seen businesses rushing to adopt new technologies for fear of being left behind. "However, we’ve seen recurring problems. These often substantial investments in tech lack clear forecasts of returns or the financial management frameworks needed to oversee and manage them. [...] Skip to main Mail Advertisement Advertisement Return to homepage # James Todd & Co warn of financial blind spots in consultancy sector Daily Echo 3 min read Add Yahoo as a preferred source to see more of our stories on Google. James Todd & Co, a long-established team of Chartered Accountants, advisers and business mentors based in Chichester and Fareham, has commented on some of the core financial management issues that consulting and professional services firms face, which are causing ongoing problems for sector resilience and profitability.

title: Startups lead hiring at 8.9% Net Employment Change, BPO lowest at 0.1%: TeamLease report - Storyboard18
summary: Capability-led expansion across sectors Hiring in these sectors is increasingly focused on specialised roles. In startups, demand is concentrated in product engineering, supply chain optimisation and data-driven growth roles. Healthcare is expanding across clinical services, regulatory functions and digital health, while manufacturing is seeing demand for automation, semiconductor and advanced engineering roles. At the same time, the report noted that hiring is no longer volume-driven but aligned with capability depth and productivity. Moderation after high-growth phases Several sectors showed moderated growth after earlier expansion. Logistics, retail, FMCG and automotive all recorded positive but slower hiring momentum, reflecting calibrated workforce alignment. [...] Healthcare and pharmaceuticals followed with 7.0%, while manufacturing, engineering and infrastructure posted 6.6%, reflecting expansion driven by digital commerce, healthcare demand and industrial capex cycles. The report showed strong hiring intent across these sectors, with 80% of startups, 78% of healthcare firms, and 70% of manufacturing companies planning workforce expansion. title: SK hynix writes down Gauss Labs as Nvidia alliance drives AI manufacturing push - CHOSUNBIZ - Chosunbiz
summary: Gauss Labs' only de facto client is its parent, SK hynix. Its footing is said to be wavering as SK Group teamed up with Nvidia and began building a "manufacturing AI cloud" in Oct. last year. SK hynix recently said it would use Nvidia's digital twin platform Omniverse to implement an "autonomous factory" by 2030. The goal is to build, together with Nvidia, a manufacturing site where AI understands the field and executes optimal decisions on its own. [...] Still, inside and outside SK hynix, some say Gauss Labs' results so far have remained at the research and development (R&D) level. The limitation, they note, is that without business results, it is hard to say it performed as a corporation, and it effectively stayed at the level of an "in-house research organization of SK hynix." Gauss Labs has never posted a net profit since its launch. From 2021 through the end of last year, its cumulative net loss totaled 18,958,000,000 won. It also failed to secure clients. Early on, it planned to expand not only into SK Group's manufacturing affiliates such as energy and biotech, but also into the global market. Currently, however, it has no clear clients other than SK hynix. [...] SK hynix's equity stake has inched down as Gauss Labs employees exercised stock options. Even so, the book value did not change for about three years after the capital contribution was completed. One interpretation is that the book value could be maintained because Gauss Labs actually helped improve manufacturing efficiency in SK hynix's semiconductor processes. ◇ Cumulative net loss of 19,000,000,000 won… business results fall short of expectations

Stands Up to Iran #### The Social-Media Shakedown Begins [...] 0700 GMT – China Telecom earnings face a bleak outlook, says Morningstar’s Dan Baker in a note. The company’s increased expenses dragged its 2025 Ebitda and net profit lower despite a flattish revenue, he notes. Industrial digitization, which used to be the Chinese telecommunications operator’s growth engine, showed a small top line decline, which appears worrying, he adds. A 3.0% value-added tax increase is poised to come into effect soon. This is likely to weigh on China Telecom’s earnings, the analyst notes, adding that he expects the company’s net profit to fall 9.0% in 2026 and cuts his near-term earnings projections by 15%-20%. Morningstar lowers its fair-value estimate by 15% to HK$5.80. Shares fall 0.4% to HK$4.89. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

title: Singapore: Claims for domestic general insurance segment rose 8.7% y-o-y in 2025 - Asia Insurance Review
summary: Road safety remains a commitment for the sector, and the GIA noted in its statement that it will continue to work closely alongside the Traffic Police and other stakeholders to support initiatives aimed at reducing dangerous driving behaviours and improving road safety. Property insurance premiums reached S$864.1m in 2025, a 4.1% y-o-y increase, even as claims increased by 60.5%, due to a rise in fire incidents. Health insurance continued its growth trajectory, with premiums rising 7.4% y-o-y to S$1.24bn. Overall health insurance claims incurred increased 6.4% to S$409.4m, reinforcing the broader trend of increasing healthcare needs as well as higher treatment costs driven by medical inflation. [...] The increase in claims was observed across several domestic business segments, including motor and property insurance. Motor and property Motor claims recorded one of the largest increases, climbing 11% despite the number of motor accidents recorded by GIA remaining stable. This suggests increasing severity of motor accidents with the number of road traffic fatalities hitting a 10-year high in 2025. Property claims also rose, shaped in part by a higher incidence of fire incidents across residential and commercial properties, alongside several large property-related losses. Statistics from the Singapore Civil Defence Force showed that the overall number of fires increased 3% to 2,050 incidents in 2025. title: South Korea's Overseas Investment Rebounds 8.7% After Two-Year Decline - 조선일보
summary: 2 Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix Plunge Amid TurboQuant, Iran War Fears 3 Google's TurboQuant Sparks Semiconductor Stock Plunge 4 Song Ji-hyo Halts YouTube Amid 'Running Man' Controversy 5 Tonight Show Host's North Korea Joke Angers BTS Fans 6 South Korean Universities Decline in Computer Science, AI Rankings 7 Fire at Yeongdeok Wind Power Complex Kills Three Maintenance Workers 8 Cuba's Blackouts Force Residents to Use Phone Lights 9 South Korea Introduces Separate Taxation for High-Dividend Stocks 10 Ukraine Deploys Humanoid Robots in Battlefield Reconnaissance 6 South Korean Universities Decline in Computer Science, AI Rankings 7 Fire at Yeongdeok Wind Power Complex Kills Three Maintenance Workers 8 Cuba's Blackouts Force Residents to Use Phone Lights 9 [...] # South Korea's Overseas Investment Up 8.7% After Two-Year Decline . After hitting a record high of 83.48 billion USD in 2022, it decreased for two consecutive years before rebounding. The net investment amount, calculated by subtracting recovered funds (from equity sales, liquidations, etc.) from total investment, fell by 12.9% to 42.5 billion USD. Peaking at 66.26 billion USD in 2022, the net investment amount has now declined for three straight years, marking the lowest level since 2017 (35.52 billion USD). This drop coincided with a surge in recovered funds, which jumped by over 65% from 18.27 billion USD the previous year to 30.19 billion USD last year. [...] The real estate sector plummeted by 46.9% to 3 billion USD from the previous year (5.65 billion USD). A source from the Ministry of Finance and Economy explained, "This is related to the significant underperformance of the U.S. commercial real estate sector in recent years." The mining sector also saw a double-digit decline of 41.6%. By country, investment in the U.S. increased by 12.9% to 25.27 billion USD from the previous year (22.38 billion USD). The Ministry of Finance and Economy noted that U.S.-bound investment "rose for the first time since 2022." While financial and insurance sector investment grew from 9.8 billion USD to 13 billion USD, manufacturing investment remained flat at 6.6 billion USD.
